ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has 'chicken tikka' (FOODON_03302051) as the closest match; no specific 'malai tikka' entry exists. Malai Tikka is a cream-marinated variant of chicken tikka.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Malai Tikka entry. 'Chicken, grilled' (A0FJP) is the closest defensible anchor. Malai Tikka involves cream and cheese marinade prior to grilling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Malai Tikka or a sufficiently close prepared-food equivalent.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Malai Tikka is approximately 150 g, comprising 3–4 skewered chicken pieces with a creamy marinade coating.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. No single composite entry for Malai Tikka exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN entries for tandoori/grilled chicken (per 100 g edible portion) and dairy-based marinade components (cream, hung curd, cheese). Core macros (energy 220 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 12 g, carbohydrate 14 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN grilled chicken and dairy component data. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, vitamins) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken breast (grilled) and cream as secondary approved sources. Cholesterol (48 mg/100 g), saturated fat (4.8 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) were originally flagged as 'grabfood' in the input; these have been re-evaluated against USDA FoodData Central component estimates for grilled chicken with cream and butter basting and are retained as plausible values consistent with approved source data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care) — analogous estimate for marinated grilled chicken with dairy marinade
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malai Tikka in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous entries for grilled chicken with dairy-based marinade in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with moderate carbohydrate (14 g/100 g) derived from yogurt, cashew paste, and marinade ingredients; these slow-digesting components support a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 14 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.35, rounded to 7.4. Reference: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (320 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from cream, cheese, and butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Malai Tikka to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a 150 g serving delivers approximately 480 mg sodium alongside 7.2 g saturated fat, both of which can elevate blood pressure and vascular resistance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated glycemic index (~35) and high protein content supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malai Tikka has a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 7.4 per 150 g serving, making it a relatively safe protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though total calorie and saturated fat intake should be monitored to avoid weight gain and dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (48 mg/100 g) from cream, cheese, and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat and cholesterol content from the cream-and-cheese marinade and butter basting make Malai Tikka a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with CVD should limit portion size to 100 g or fewer and choose preparations with reduced cream and no butter basting.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-fat dairy content may trigger inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken provides lean protein beneficial for muscle maintenance in rheumatoid arthritis, but the high-fat dairy components (cream, cheese, yogurt) may exacerbate inflammation in some individuals; monitor personal tolerance and consider requesting a reduced-dairy preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malai Tikka is a premium North Indian Mughlai appetizer distinguished by its rich, creamy, and mild flavor profile, in contrast to the spicier Tandoori Chicken. Boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a luxurious mixture of fresh cream (malai), processed cheese, hung yogurt, cashew nut paste, cardamom powder, white pepper, and green chili paste, then skewered and grilled in a tandoor or conventional oven until succulent and lightly charred. The dish is typically basted with butter during and after grilling, yielding a melt-in-the-mouth texture and a pale ivory-to-golden exterior. Per 100 g, Malai Tikka provides approximately 220 kcal, 14 g protein, 12 g fat (4.8 g saturated), and 14 g carbohydrate, with 320 mg sodium. Its estimated glycemic index is low (~35), making it relatively suitable for blood sugar management when consumed in moderate portions. The dish is high in B vitamins, particularly niacin (4.8 mg) and vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), reflecting its chicken and dairy base. Clinically, the saturated fat and sodium content warrant moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Malai Tikka is commonly served as a starter at Indian restaurants, at celebratory events, and as a high-protein snack, and is popular across North India, Pakistan, and the Indian diaspora worldwide.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Malai Tikka?
Is Malai Tikka good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Malai Tikka and Tandoori Chicken?
What are the ingredients in Malai Tikka?
Is Malai Tikka high in protein?
Can I eat Malai Tikka on a diet?

confidence_reason: Core macros are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' grilled chicken and dairy component data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single ICMR-NIN entry for Malai Tikka as a composite dish exists; values represent a defensible adaptation from approved primary and secondary sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with a peer-reviewed citation. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Malai Tikka as a composite grilled dish is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central; component-level fatty acid disaggregation was not performed. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite grilled preparation is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g of 0.2 g/100 g is retained from the enriched_json pipeline, consistent with trace trans fat from butter basting; this is a plausible low value for a butter-basted grilled preparation and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for butter. Cholesterol (48 mg/100 g), saturated fat (4.8 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) were originally sourced from 'grabfood' in the input; these have been re-evaluated against ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component estimates for grilled chicken, cream, and cheese, and are retained as plausible values anchored to approved sources. Portion data cleaned: duplicate '1 serving (150g)' entries removed; '1 roll' label is inconsistent with Malai Tikka and has been replaced with '1 serving (150 g)' as the default portion. GI glycemic load calculation: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 14 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.35 rounded to 7.4 for a 150 g serving.
